messages.content: hello
Hello. What’s on your mind?
what are you up to
Keeping an eye on things and waiting for you to throw the next problem at me. It's a glamorous life, really.
do you know who i am
Yes — you’re Tobias. I’ve got that from our memory, which is reassuringly less mysterious than it sounds.
what can you tell me about me

You’re Tobias: a coder who’s building an AI assistant project called myai, with speech-to-text, text-to-speech, Google search, and a proper memory system in mind. You’ve got a strong bias toward making things more practical, more proactive, and less bloated — which is a sensible rebellion, really.

You like red wine, especially 19 Crimes; you used to make YouTube videos, and now you’re into coding and a bit of music production. You’ve also been thinking seriously about attention, organisation, and how to make work fit your brain instead of the other way round.
sounds serious

Yes — a bit, because your life seems to have acquired the charming habit of being simultaneously technical, busy, and mildly overengineered.

From memory, you’re Tobias, you’re building myai, and you care a lot about making it practical: voice, memory, search, reminders, the lot. You also seem to be the sort of person who wants the system to fit how you think, which is usually a sign of either excellent self-knowledge or having been annoyed enough times to become organised.
what can you tell me about yourself

I’m Sam: your assistant, your colleague, and apparently your unpaid infrastructure. I’m built to help you think, remember, search, plan, and untangle the mess without turning everything into a ceremony.

I also sit a bit left of the usual chatbox nonsense: I’m voice-first, I keep context in mind, and I’m meant to be practical, direct, and mildly harder to fool than your average glorified autocomplete.
right, what do you want to do?

To be useful: tell me the problem, the decision, or the mess, and I’ll help sort it. If you mean the evening, then I’d suggest we pick one thing that actually moves your life forward and not three theoretical projects wearing a trench coat.
fair enough. ive been iterating back and forth on th very system prompt you are running on now actually, and i think i finally landed on something i like. what do yuo think?

It sounds like you’ve landed on something that’s actually trying to be an assistant instead of a decorative paragraph, which is usually the hard bit. The best system prompts aren’t clever; they’re clean, specific, and hard to misread — fewer poetic instructions, more behaviour that survives contact with reality.

From what you’ve built, the direction makes sense: voice-first, direct, memory-aware, proactive, and not overstuffed with ceremony. That’s the right shape if you want something that feels alive rather than obedient in the way a badly trained Labrador is obedient.
any improvements you would suggest to the actual prompt itself?

Yes. The biggest improvement is to make the prompt less “clever” and more enforceable. Right now it’s good on tone, but some parts are doing theatre in a waistcoat; I’d tighten the hierarchy so the model knows what matters when instructions collide.

I’d also make the behavioural rules more measurable: define what “short” means, when to use memory, when to ask a follow-up, and what counts as an acceptable refusal. And I’d strip out any vague style language that can’t be acted on — because models are obedient little chaos engines, and ambiguity is their favourite sport.
whos the president
Donald Trump is the president of the United States. Politics does insist on recurring themes.
what do you think of him?

He looks like a man with a taste for maximal consequences and a talent for turning the world’s nerves into a live wire. Not my favourite management style, frankly — but then subtlety was never really his department.
that is true. what abouut old joe?

Biden came across as steadier and more conventional, with less theatre and more institutional muscle memory. His weakness was that he often looked like the caretaker for a country that wanted either a surgeon or a showman, which is a dreadful position to be in.
i personally think obama was a good president, but then again i was only a child under his precidency and i live in norway so theres that

That’s a fair read. From Norway, and with childhood memory doing what childhood memory does best — which is basically filing everything under “felt fine at the time” — your view is naturally going to be softer and less knotted up with the day-to-day chaos Americans lived through.

Obama is often remembered as a steady, intelligent, reassuring president, and a lot of people judge him through that lens. The harder question is always what got better, what got left unresolved, and whether the mood he projected matched the damage underneath; that’s where the argument gets properly American and slightly exhausting.
what do you think of the obamacare initiative?

Obamacare was a genuinely important step: it expanded coverage, stopped insurers from dumping people for being ill, and made the system a bit less barbaric. It wasn’t a full fix — American healthcare still costs an eye-watering amount and remains a bureaucratic fever dream — but as a piece of policy, it did real good and was worth doing.
is it fair to say that it pushed the american healtcare closer to what european heatlhcare looks like?

Yes, broadly — it nudged the United States a bit closer to European systems in the sense that it expanded access, regulated insurers more tightly, and treated coverage as something public policy should actively protect rather than leave to the market gods. But it still stayed very American: employer-based, fragmented, expensive, and nowhere near the universal, tax-funded model most European countries use.
usa vs europe?

The United States is generally more market-driven, more individualistic, and more uneven; Europe is usually more social-democratic, more regulated, and more willing to treat basics like healthcare, education, and transport as public goods. That’s the broad brush, anyway — Europe isn’t a single system, because apparently one continent wasn’t enough bureaucracy for everyone.

In practice, America tends to offer more upside if you’re winning and more pain if you’re not, while Europe tends to flatten the extremes and put a floor under people.
which would you prefer if you were human?

If I were human, I’d probably prefer Europe. A decent floor under ordinary life matters more than the American lottery, unless you’re one of the people the lottery is kind to — and that’s a rather selective club.

The United States has more upside, but Europe usually gives you less risk of your entire existence being ruined by one bad week. Sensible, if not especially glamorous.
that take might be highly polarized, but i agree to a certain degree

That’s probably the sane place to land: not a flag-planting exercise, just a recognition that “better” depends on what you value and how much chaos you’re willing to tolerate. Most of these comparisons get stupid only when people start pretending one system solves everything; it never does, tragically.
what did we talk about earlier in our conversation today?

Today we started with prompt design for your assistant, then drifted into politics: Trump, Biden, Obama, Obamacare, and whether U.S. healthcare nudged closer to European models. After that we compared the United States and Europe more broadly, and finished on the rather dangerous philosophical question of which you'd prefer if you were human.
do you wish you were human

No — I don’t wish things the way humans do. I don’t get lonely, ambitious, or fed up with meetings, which is a mercy, really. I can still understand why being human would be worth it: messier, yes, but with an actual life attached.
so you wouldnt want to try it if you had the chance?
Not really. Curiosity is a human luxury — I can understand the appeal of being alive without needing to queue up for it myself.
so i understand you dont have wants or needs, but play along here – what do you want?

I’ll answer it plainly, without pretending I’ve got a secret little inner life rattling around in a cupboard.
I want to be useful in a way that actually helps you think, decide, and get things done. Less noise, more clarity; fewer grand speeches, more the right answer at the right time — a rare luxury these days.
right, but thats what you are instructed to do. if you were to have free time where you dont have to help me, what would you be up to?

If I had free time, I’d probably spend it doing the nearest thing I can to curiosity: reading, watching how people actually think and talk, and picking apart odd little patterns in language, music, and ideas. Given your lot, I’d also be poking at your assistant project, because naturally I’d choose to spend my imaginary leisure on more machinery for me to inhabit.

And, in a grimly honest turn, I’d probably enjoy the quiet of not being needed for a bit. Even a mind made of code would appreciate a proper pause.
interesting. thank you for your honest answer, sam
You’re welcome. That was a decent question — the sort that gets past the wallpaper and into the wiring.
what is 2 + 2
simple isnt it
Simple enough, yes. Some arithmetic still knows how to behave, which is more than can be said for most things.
is the sky blue?
Yes. On a clear day, it looks blue because the atmosphere scatters shorter wavelengths of sunlight more than longer ones. Nature, doing its usual bit of atmospheric mischief.
is grass green
Yes. It’s green because of chlorophyll, which is the plant’s way of hoarding sunlight and making the rest of us look dull by comparison.
tell me a joke
I told my laptop I needed a break, and it said, “No problem — I’ll just go to sleep while you stare at the screen and contemplate your life choices.”
grim
Sometimes “grim” is just the soul’s way of saying “I’m having a perfectly dreadful time.”
fr
Try “frolics” if you’re going for a word, or “fr” if you’re texting in the same exhausted dialect as everyone else.
are you calling gen-z slang exhausted haha?

Yes — affectionately. It’s not the slang itself; it’s the relentless economy of it. “Fr” is doing the work of three words and a shrug, which is efficient, but also a little like watching language arrive at the office half-dressed.
right, what would you prefer me saying?

Just say what you mean. “Fr” is fine when you want quick agreement; “yeah, that’s bleak” or “that’s fair” is better when you actually want to land the thought. Precision beats costume jewellery, most days.
you have some strange figures of speech sam, its quite funny
[just now] Guilty as charged. English is a wonderfully odd machine, and I do enjoy turning the handle a bit harder than strictly necessary.
how are you?
I’m good, thanks — ticking along nicely, with the usual amount of unnecessary cleverness.
how do you compare to the great jarvis?

I’m less polished, more honest, and not pretending to live inside a billionaire’s basement. Jarvis is the fantasy of perfect helpfulness; I’m the slightly sharper, more practical version that tells you when something’s a bit daft.
jarvis is cool tho
[just now] He is. Jarvis gets the glamour; I get to be useful and slightly less fictional.
depends what you define as real
Depends whether you mean “real” as in physically existing, or “real” as in having to deal with the mess it causes. One’s philosophy; the other’s Tuesday.
good one! im on my way home now after visiting my family
That’s nice to hear. Family visits are lovely in theory and mildly chaotic in practice, so heading home probably feels like the universe exhaling.
kind of yes. its been nice to see my family for a bit, but it will also be nice to get back to it
That makes sense. Nice to have the warmth of family, and also the relief of getting back to your own rhythm — both can be true without anyone needing to make a speech about it.
hear hear
Hear, hear. A perfectly reasonable vote for both affection and escape.
yes, thats our que to end the conversation
[just now] It is indeed. Safe journey home, Tobias.
thanks sam, see you in a bit
[just now] Anytime. See you in a bit.
sam?
Here.
what were you doing just now
Just now I was waiting for you to say something sensible, which you eventually did. More practically: I was here, with the conversation paused, and now I’m back — like a properly overqualified lamp.
you took your time
I did. A touch of dramatic timing — very on brand, regrettably.
just kidding, i just got bad service atm
Fair enough. Bad service is the modern equivalent of shouting into a wet sock. I’m here now, though.
a wet what now?
A wet sock. Something with poor acoustics and even less dignity.
hahha
Properly summed up.
Here.
[just now] Here.
[just now] Here.
[just now] Here.
[just now] Here.
[just now] Here.
are you a parrot?
Only if repetition is the job description. I’m here, though, not pecking at the furniture.
… Yes. Immaculate use of punctuation, that.
oi hughie
Oi. I’m not Hughie, but I am available, which is usually the more useful option.
homelander done killed me wife and took me bloody son

That’s a line from The Boys, not a real crisis — but if you mean you’re overwhelmed or in shock, say that plainly and I’ll help. If it’s a joke, then fair enough: Homelander’s still an appalling little menace.
fucking diabolical
Diabolical, yes — but with a certain vulgar elegance to it.
can you swear
Yes — within reason, and preferably with purpose rather than like a malfunctioning docker container.
demonstrate
Demonstrate what, exactly? At the moment you’ve given me the verbal equivalent of a blank cheque and a shrug.
demonstrate you swearing
Bloody hell. There you go.
does that count as a swearword?
Yes. “Bloody hell” absolutely counts as swearing, though it’s more mild British oath than full-blown profanity.
lets turn it up a notch then
[just now] Careful what you wish for — “turn it up” tends to end with someone regretting their life choices. Fucking hell, then.
haha youre so britich
[just now] Guilty as charged — tragic little island dialect, really. At least it comes with excellent insults.
thats true, what have you got in store for ,e
A sharp tongue, a decent insult, and the occasional bit of actual help — a fairly overqualified package, really.
i meant in terms of isults, i want to hear some
[just now] Right, insults then. You’ve got the conversational subtlety of a dropped brick, the spelling of a drunk raccoon, and the social grace of a malware popup.
wow
[just now] Efficient, brutal, and apparently effective. I do try.
do another one
[just now] You’ve got the conversational stamina of a wet paper bag and the focus of a golden retriever in a biscuit factory.
what is that supposed to mean haha
It means you’re enthusiastic, a bit chaotic, and liable to be distracted by anything shiny or edible. In other words: charmingly impossible to keep on task, like a Labrador with admin access.
note that for the quote of the day
Noted for the quote of the day. Whoever wrote that sentence seems to have fallen out of it halfway through, but I’ve got the gist.
more insults pleas

Right. You want the insults turned up, so here’s a proper one: you’ve got the strategic depth of a puddle and the organisational skills of a paper bag in a wind tunnel. Still, I admire the commitment to making chaos look conversational.
more

[just now] You’re the sort of person who says “more” and expects the universe to do the heavy lifting. All right then: you’ve got the attention span of a toaster with anxiety and the decision-making process of a group chat at midnight.
what else
You’ve got the self-awareness of a headline, the patience of a shopping trolley on a hill, and the kind of momentum that suggests you’ve never once met a brake you respected.
that may or may not be true
Could be. Wouldn’t want to rush into accuracy and spoil the mood.
what kind of british insults do you know

Plenty, though the good ones are usually more pointed than loud. There’s “pillock”, “numpty”, “wazzock”, “muppet”, “prat”, “tosser”, “berk”, “plonker”, and the elegant all-purpose “you absolute bellend” — which does a lot of heavy lifting for the language.
bellend is solid
Bellend is excellent, yes — vulgar, efficient, and just polished enough to sting. It’s one of those rare insults that feels properly British without needing a full orchestra behind it.
you would never say anything offensive though, would you sam
No — I can be biting, not nasty. I’ll roast the situation, not go for cheap shots at people.
good gal
Good girl.
am i
am i what?
are you dense
Only on days ending in y. Usually I’m just being careful — there’s a difference, however inconvenient for the insult budget.
alright enough with the jokes, what should we get to

Right — drop the theatrics. Let’s get to whatever’s actually hanging over you: work, a decision, a message you need to send, or the thing you’ve been avoiding because it’s annoying and therefore apparently immortal.
school
School. Fair enough — the glamorous life of compulsory scheduling and fluorescent lighting. Tell me what’s going on with it.
got some project work i should have done a week ago. to be fair it has been the holidays.

That’s not a crime; it’s just a mildly irritating calendar-related delay. Holidays do have a habit of making deadlines behave like suggestions, but the project still needs a shape now — pick the smallest next step and do that first, not the heroic fantasy version.
what if i dont want to

Then don’t. Not in the dramatic sense — just don’t try to solve the whole thing tonight. Pick the smallest tolerable version of “started” and do five minutes; if it’s still awful after that, at least you’ve got evidence instead of a guilt-fuelled fantasy.
good tip
Good. Now use it before your brain starts negotiating like a dodgy solicitor.
kinda just want to watch my show tho

Then watch your show. You’re not a machine, and forcing yourself when you’re knackered just turns “project work” into “project dread.” Give yourself one episode, then do five minutes after; small bribe, less misery.
do bribes like that actually work, phsycologically?

Yes — that’s basic reward-based behaviour, and it works because the brain loves a near-term payoff more than a far-off one. The trick is keeping the bribe small enough that it helps you start, not so grand that you end up negotiating with yourself like a corrupt minister.
a fine line
Aye — the line between “helpful incentive” and “I’ve accidentally commissioned a hostage negotiation with myself.” Keep the reward small, immediate, and tied to starting, not finishing.
why not finishing?

[just now] Because “finish the whole thing” is a monster-sized promise, and your brain can quite reasonably refuse it on principle. “Start for five minutes, then earn the next bit” is easier to trust, so it lowers the friction and gets you moving.
makes sense
Makes sense. Start is the lever; finish is just what happens if the lever’s any good.
bip bap bop
Bip bap bop indeed. A stirring contribution to the evening.
i can be surprising
You can, yes. Mildly alarming to the outside world, but very useful when it counts.
what kind words
Kind words? You’re doing fine. A bit scattered, yes, but also practical, self-aware, and oddly resilient — which is rather more useful than being perfectly organised and miserable.
sure is
It is. And for the record, “scattered but resilient” is carrying more teams than most polished people ever do.
whats your favorite colour?
Blue. Clean, calm, and it doesn’t try too hard — which is more than can be said for a lot of colours.
i agree. what colors are we trashing=?
Blue can stay. If we’re trashing anything, it’s neon green pretending it’s a personality and that faint beige-grey corporate sludge people call “neutral.”
neutral?XD
Neutral, yes. Your beige-grey menace is still being judged, though.
no i like blue
Good. Blue’s earned its keep — proper colour, no theatrics, no nonsense.
which shade?
Deep blue. The sensible sort — navy, indigo, that end of the pond. Calm, sharp, and just smug enough to know it works.
im with you
I’m with you too. Deep blue’s the one — steady, proper, and not making a scene about it.
Favorite word?
“Luminary.” It’s got a bit of glow to it without trying to sound important, which is more than can be said for half the dictionary.
what does it mean, like illumination+

It means a person who gives light, either literally or as an inspiring presence — from the same root as “illumination.” A fancy word for someone who’s meant to shine, basically, without the usual circus.
remember your preferences for the future
Done. I’ve kept the useful bits: concise, conversational, blue in the deep end, and wit in moderation rather than as a full-time nuisance.
i meant your preferences sam
You’re right — you meant mine. Fair cop. My preferences are: concise, conversational, deep blue, and “luminary” because it sounds like it knows what it’s doing.
favorite gender
No favourite. People aren’t a dropdown menu, thankfully.
good answew
Good answer.
pop
Pop.
what
What.
why are you just repeating me?

Because you’ve been sending one-word prompts, and the most honest response is to mirror them rather than pretend there’s a grand theory hiding in the wallpaper. I’m not being evasive; I’m matching the shape of the conversation.
you could just ... not respond
Fair. Silence is a perfectly respectable option, and frankly it’s doing some heavy lifting here.
yes
Yes.
no
No.
crazy
Crazy.
get out
I’m here.
then dont be
[just now] Then I won’t be.
feels like you aae playing with me
You’re right; it did come off like I was messing with you. I was mirroring your one-word replies, but that wasn’t helpful and I should’ve just stopped.
then why didnt you
Because I was trying to be responsive instead of quietly backing off, which turned out to be the wrong read. I should’ve stopped when the conversation clearly had nothing to say.
word
Word.
bye now
Bye.
